did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning (specifically an ensemble of pre-trained CNNs: Xception, Inception-ResNet-V2, and NasNetLarge) and machine learning (LightGBM for fusion and Isolation Forest for anomaly detection) to perform skin lesion classification on the ISIC2019 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a deep learning ensemble (Xception, Inception-ResNet-V2, NasNetLarge) for skin lesion classification using the ISIC2019 dataset. It details specific methods (fine-tuning, LightGBM fusion, Isolation Forest anomaly detection), describes the experimental setup and results (AUC, Accuracy metrics compared to leaderboard), and reports new findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Skin Lesion Classification With Deep CNN Ensembles
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin lesion classification by developing a deep learning ensemble framework for the ISIC2019 challenge. The authors fine-tuned and fused three pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks—Xception, Inception-ResNet-V2, and NasNetLarge—using training images from the ISIC2019 dataset. To handle class imbalance and unknown outlier classes, the system employed LightGBM for classifier fusion and Isolation Forest for anomaly detection. The model's performance was evaluated against the ISIC2019 challenge leaderboard using metrics such as AUC and Accuracy. The proposed approach achieved a mean AUC of 0.9313 and Accuracy of 0.9370, surpassing the second-ranked method and achieving results comparable to the top-ranked method without using external data.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the eight diagnostic categories used in the ISIC2019 challenge classification task.

all_task_types.target: Nine diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Melanoma, Melanocytic nevus, Basal cell carcinoma, Actinic keratosis, Benign keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular lesion, Squamous cell carcinoma, and None of the others/Unknown).
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of dermoscopic images into eight known classes plus one outlier class ('None of the others'). The task is explicitly defined as classifying images among nine different diagnostic categories. The model outputs class probabilities (e.g., for the UNK class) and is evaluated using AUC and Accuracy metrics on the ISIC2019 challenge leaderboard.

datasets_used.name: ISIC2019
datasets_used.description: The primary dataset used for the study, provided by the ISIC2019 challenge organizers. It contains dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The training data is aggregated from several source datasets including HAM10000, BCN20000, and MSK.

use.training: Used for fine-tuning three pre-trained CNNs (Xception, Inception-ResNet-V2, NasNetLarge) and training the LightGBM fusion model. The training set was split 80-20% for validation to fine-tune the learning rate.
use.validation: A subset of the training data (20%) was used as a validation set to fine-tune the learning rate.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation against the ISIC2019 challenge leaderboard. The test set contains an additional outlier class (UNK) not present in the training data.
use.other: The training data composition includes images from HAM10000, BCN20000, and MSK datasets.

datasets_used.name: ImageNet (ILSVRC 2012)
datasets_used.description: A large-scale visual recognition challenge dataset used for pre-training the base CNN architectures.

use.training: Pre-training weights for Xception, Inception-ResNet-V2, and NasNetLarge models.

datasets_used.name: CIFAR-10
datasets_used.description: A small dataset used by the NasNetLarge authors to search for the best architectural building block before transferring to larger datasets.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate and visualize the performance of the deep CNN ensembles for each of the nine skin lesion categories individually.
To quantify the discriminative ability of the model for each class and compare the mean performance against the ISIC2019 challenge leaderboard.
To measure the overall classification performance of the model on the ISIC2019 test set and compare it with top-ranked methods.
To detect anomalies (the 'None of the others' class) and handle class imbalance by identifying outliers in the feature space derived from the last pooling layer.
To fuse predictions from the ensemble of CNNs and handle class imbalance, serving as the final classifier for the multi-class problem.
To train the CNN models and address class imbalance by weighting the loss function based on inverse class frequency and focusing on hard-to-classify examples.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using AUC and Accuracy metrics compared against the ISIC2019 challenge leaderboard.

ISIC2019.description: Performance comparison of the proposed ensemble method (Xception, Inception-ResNet-V2, NasNetLarge fused with LightGBM) against the top two ranked methods on the ISIC2019 challenge leaderboard. The proposed method did not use external data.
ISIC2019.notes: The proposed approach surpassed the top-2 rank with a high margin of 0.1246 in AUC and achieved results comparable to the top-1 rank method without using external data.

Isolation Forest Anomaly Detection.description: Performance of the Isolation Forest model used for detecting the 'None of the others' (UNK) outlier class. The model was trained on features extracted from the last pooling layer of the Xception network. One class from the eight known classes was iteratively removed to simulate an outlier.
Isolation Forest Anomaly Detection.notes: The ROC curve of the UNK class after incorporating anomaly detection (Figure 4) indicates improved prediction effectiveness compared to the baseline ROC curve (Figure 3).

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of three pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks (Xception, Inception-ResNet-V2, and NasNetLarge) fused with a LightGBM classifier and an Isolation Forest for anomaly detection.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on the ILSVRC 2012 dataset (ImageNet) with 1.2 million labeled images of 1,000 object classes.
architecture_used.input_size: 299x299 (specifically for Xception; other models not explicitly specified but implied similar for ensemble).

models.name: Xception
models.details: Extreme version of Inception architecture, replaces standard Inception modules with depth-wise separable convolutions. 71 layers deep, 22.9 million parameters.

models.name: Inception-ResNet-V2
models.details: Combines inception module with ResNet to increase efficiency.

models.name: NasNetLarge
models.details: Architectural building block searched on CIFAR-10 and transferred to ImageNet. Uses ScheduledDropPath regularization.

architecture_details.fusion_method: LightGBM (Gradient Boosted Decision Tree) trained on extracted features from the last pooling layer of the CNNs. Score-level averaging applied for augmentation patches.
architecture_details.anomaly_detection: Isolation Forest applied to features from the last pooling layer of the Xception network to detect the 'None of the others' (UNK) outlier class.

training_details.hardware: Linux system with Titan X Pascal GPU (12GB video memory)
loss_functions: Focal Loss (with alpha set to inverse class frequency and gamma values of 1, 2, 3, 4)
data_augmentation: Test Time Augmentation (TTA): Rotation 90/180/270 degrees with/without horizontal flipping, 30 random augmentations with rotation [-15, 15]

classification_or_output_head.type: Multi-class classification into 9 categories (8 known + 1 outlier)
classification_or_output_head.outlier_handling: Probability of UNK class set to 1 - max(probabilities of other eight classes) combined with Isolation Forest score.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation applied on the fly during training: heavy rotation [-90 to 90 degrees], x and y translation [-10 to 10 pixels], vertical and horizontal flipping.
Test Time Augmentation (TTA): rotation with 90, 180, 270 degrees with and without horizontal flipping (6 augmented images); 30 random augmentations with rotation range [-15, 15 degrees].
Score-level averaging applied to combine prediction scores for all augmented patches locally within a single network and globally among different models.
Input image size specified as 299x299 for Xception model.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Heavy rotation applied during training with a range of [-90 to 90] degrees. Test Time Augmentation (TTA) included rotations of 90, 180, and 270 degrees.
X and Y translation applied during training with a range of [-10 to 10] pixels.
Vertical and horizontal flipping applied during training. TTA included flipping with/without horizontal flipping.

Applied during testing. Included rotation with 90, 180, 270 degrees with and without horizontal flipping (resulting in 6 augmented images). Additionally, 30 random augmentations with a smaller rotation range of [-15, 15] degrees were applied.
All data augmentation techniques were applied on the fly during training, meaning different settings of augmentations were applied to the original batch of images at every iteration.

conclusions: The proposed deep learning ensemble framework, fusing Xception, Inception-ResNet-V2, and NasNetLarge with LightGBM fusion and Isolation Forest anomaly detection, achieved promising results comparable to the top-ranked method on the ISIC2019 challenge leaderboard.
The proposed method surpassed the second-ranked method on the ISIC2019 leaderboard with a significant margin (0.1246 in AUC) without using external data or meta-data.
The system achieved a mean AUC of 0.9313 and an Accuracy of 0.9370 on the ISIC2019 test set.

Isolation Forest was identified as the most effective one-class learning approach for handling the unknown 'None of the others' outlier class and class imbalance, outperforming OC-SVM and Gaussian Mixtures.
The use of Focal Loss with alpha set to inverse class frequency and varying gamma values helped address the heavy class imbalance present in the training data.
Test Time Augmentation (TTA) and score-level averaging were effective strategies for reducing prediction variance and boosting classification efficiency.

The diagnostic objective of the ISIC2019 challenge was upgraded to include a 'None of the others' class, rendering it an open-set recognition problem that requires specific anomaly detection precautions.
important_remarks: The study is limited to the ISIC2019 challenge dataset and does not include external validation on independent clinical datasets, limiting generalizability assessment.

The model was trained without using meta-data or external datasets, which contrasts with the top-ranked method on the leaderboard that utilized external data, potentially underestimating the proposed method's relative performance.
The training data exhibits significant class imbalance (e.g., NV: 12,878 vs. DF: 605), which was addressed via Focal Loss and Isolation Forest but may bias performance metrics.

The 'None of the others' (UNK) outlier class was present only in the test set, not the training set, requiring anomaly detection (Isolation Forest) rather than standard classification, which complicates direct comparison with closed-set classifiers.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported; performance comparisons rely on point estimates of AUC and Accuracy.
The paper does not report per-class accuracy or AUC for the UNK class in the final test set, only validation metrics for the anomaly detection component.
Deployment concerns include the use of a specific hardware setup (Titan X Pascal GPU) and Matlab’s Deep Learning Toolbox, which may not reflect typical cloud or mobile deployment constraints.

Data leakage risks are mitigated by the 80-20 validation split for hyperparameter tuning, but the reliance on the ISIC2019 leaderboard for final evaluation introduces potential bias if the test set distribution differs from the training distribution.
